basmala: In the Name of God, the Infinitely Merciful, the Most Merciful.
intro: Al-Ikhlāṣ — “Sincerity”, or “the Purity of Faith” — is the shortest affirmation of Divine Unity in the Quran. But its title, ikhlāṣ (إِخْلَاص), joins two things — the sincerity of the worshipper and the purity of the Worshipped: for none can worship purely save the one who acknowledges purely. Tustarī, asked what sincerity is, answered with a word that almost sounds the same: iflās (إِفْلَاس), insolvency. “Whoever knows that he is insolvent has grasped the truth of his situation.” Ikhlāṣ begins there — in the recognition that one has nothing of one's own. Qushayrī opens another window: the four verses, he says, address four degrees of soul at once — Huwa (He) calls those absorbed in love; Allāh calls the unifiers; Aḥad (One) calls the gnostics; al-Ṣamad calls the learned; lam yalid wa-lam yūlad calls those of intellect. Each Name unveils a veil. The sūra is thus not only the profession of Unity: it is a graded unveiling, toward the One whom no name can contain.

versets.commentaire: Qushayrī reads the verse as a graded unveiling. Huwa (هُوَ), “He”, is more than a pronoun: for several Sufis it is the supreme Name — pure designation, before every attribute, before every saying. Some gnostics (ahl al-maʿrifa) have made it their only invocation: yā Huwa, “O He”. Then comes Allāh, the Name that gathers. Then Aḥad (أَحَد), “One” — not the oneness of counting (wāḥid), which is one in a series, but the absolute Oneness that admits neither second nor division. And Qushayrī then says a profound word: “He [Huwa] is the unveiler of secrets; Allāh, the unveiler of spirits; Aḥad, the unveiler of hearts.” Each word, as it is pronounced, draws back a veil within the reader's soul.

mots.def: to say, to utter; in the imperative: “say”, “proclaim”.
3rd person singular masculine pronoun: He; for several Sufis, the supreme Name — God named without a name, in pure otherness.
Allāh: the proper Name of God in Arabic; the “Gathering Name” (al-ism al-jāmiʿ), in which all the other Divine Names are contained.
aḥad: the absolute One, the indivisible Oneness which admits neither second nor partition; distinct from wāḥid (the one of counting).

versets.commentaire: al-Ṣamad (الصَّمَد) appears only here in the Quran. Tustarī defines it: “It is the Master (al-Sayyid) toward whom one turns for every need and every trial. Its meaning is: the One who is sought (al-Maṣmūd ilayhi). And He is the One who has no need of food or drink.” The word has two faces that meet. From one side, it says of the servant: he is what seeks, he is what needs. From the other, it says of God: He is what is sought, and He seeks nothing. Qushayrī adds: “al-Ṣamad is that which has no hollow within (la jawfa lahu), which is full, which lacks nothing.” All is in Him; and all, without Him, is mere void. When the servant, become insolvent, recognises himself as such — he turns at once toward al-Ṣamad, and finds there not a help, but the very origin of every help.

mots.def: Allāh: the proper Name of God in Arabic.
al-Ṣamad: a Divine Name unique to this sūra; lexically, what is full, imperishable, without hollow; theologically, the One toward whom every being turns in its need, on whom all things depend.

versets.trad: He begets not, nor is He begotten,
versets.commentaire: Tustarī: “He does not beget — so He has no heirs; nor is He begotten — for then His Lordship would be something that came into being in time (muḥdath). This is also the confirmation of His Oneness (waḥdāniyya), and the denial that He depends on causes (asbāb).” A deeper reading: this verse does not only refute the pagans or the Christians, it says something of God — that He is outside time, outside the chain of causes, outside every series. Nothing proceeds from anything to Him, nothing proceeds from Him as a son from a father. God is not the first cause of a series: He is the One from whom no series proceeds, because every series belongs to Him at every instant.

mots.def: negative particle: not; with the apocopated imperfect it negates a past fact.
walada: to beget, to bring forth, to give birth; with lam, absolute negation.
mots.gram: imperfect apocopated verb, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

mots.def: passive of walada: to be begotten, to be born; with lam: He has not been begotten.
mots.gram: imperfect apocopated passive, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

versets.trad: and none is like unto Him.”
versets.commentaire: “No one is comparable to Him,” Tustarī sums up. And Qushayrī, in a luminous formula, says that the verses of the sūra are the explanation of one another: Who is God? — He is God. Which Allāh? — the Unique (Aḥad). Which Unique? — al-Ṣamad. Which al-Ṣamad? — the One who does not beget nor is begotten. Which One who does not beget nor is begotten? — the One to whom none is equal. Each answer pushes the next a little further. The word aḥad closes the sūra it had opened: it is the One returning — not as a reminder, but as a final silence. At this last word, language lays down its arms: before the One, there is nothing more to say.

mots.def: kāna: to be; here in the apocopated imperfect with lam: he is not, there is not.
mots.gram: imperfect apocopated verb, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

mots.def: preposition li- + suffix pronoun 3rd pers. masc. sing.: for Him, to Him.
kufuw: equal, equivalent, comparable; what would stand opposite as a matching measure.
aḥad: one, anyone; in negative context: not one, none. The word closes the sūra it opened in v. 1.
